Use API Gateway response caching
Implement response pagination to reduce payload size
Switch to Protocol Buffers instead of JSON
explanation: CloudFront compress objects feature can automatically compress responses using gzip or Brotli when the client sends 'Accept-Encoding' header. JSON compresses very well (often 70-90% reduction). Compressed responses transfer faster, reducing latency for mobile clients. CloudFront also caches the compressed version, reducing origin bandwidth costs.

question: A company's Lambda function reads from DynamoDB and experiences throttling due to DynamoDB read capacity limits. The function reads the same small set of product catalog items repeatedly. What is the most cost-effective solution to eliminate throttling?
options: Increase DynamoDB Read Capacity Units (RCUs) with on-demand pricing
Add DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) cluster as a caching layer for the Lambda function
Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling to handle Lambda's burst reads
Use SQS to buffer Lambda requests to DynamoDB
explanation: DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is an in-memory cache for DynamoDB that provides microsecond read latency. For frequently accessed product catalog data (read many times), DAX caches the results and serves subsequent requests from memory without hitting DynamoDB. This eliminates throttling while reducing cost since cached reads don't consume DynamoDB RCUs.

question: A company needs to query large amounts of log data stored in S3 using SQL without loading data into a database. The logs are in CSV format, partitioned by date. What is the most cost-effective solution?
options: Load data into Redshift using COPY command for SQL queries
Use Athena to run SQL queries directly against S3 data
Use EMR with Hive for SQL-like queries against S3 data
Use AWS Glue ETL to transform and load into RDS for querying
explanation: Amazon Athena is serverless and allows running standard SQL queries directly against S3 data without loading it into a database. You pay only per query (per TB scanned). Using partitions (date folders) reduces the amount of data scanned per query, further reducing cost. Athena uses Glue Data Catalog for schema definition.

question: A company runs 20 t3.large EC2 instances continuously for 3 years to run a data processing application. What purchase option provides the greatest cost savings?
options: On-Demand instances with Auto Scaling to scale down during off-peak hours
3-year Reserved Instances (Standard) with full upfront payment
3-year Reserved Instances (Convertible) with partial upfront payment
Savings Plans (Compute) with 3-year commitment
explanation: 3-year Standard Reserved Instances with full upfront payment provide the maximum discount (up to 72% over On-Demand). Standard RIs provide a higher discount than Convertible RIs or Savings Plans for specific instance families. Full upfront payment provides the highest discount within the RI options. For consistent, predictable 3-year workloads, this is optimal.

question: A company stores 200TB of data in S3 Standard. Most data is accessed daily for the first 30 days, then weekly for 3 months, then rarely (once a year) after that. What S3 lifecycle configuration minimizes storage costs?
options: Transition to S3-IA after 30 days, then Glacier after 90 days
Transition to S3 Intelligent-Tiering immediately
Transition to S3-IA after 30 days, S3-IA after 90 days, Glacier Deep Archive after 180 days
Keep all data in S3 Standard with versioning disabled
explanation: The data access pattern is: daily for 30 days (S3 Standard), weekly for 3 months (S3-IA works since >30 day minimum), then rarely after that (Glacier). Transitioning to S3-IA at 30 days and Glacier at 90 days matches this pattern. S3-IA costs less than Standard but has a 30-day minimum and retrieval fee, while Glacier is much cheaper for data accessed once a year.

question: A batch processing job runs for 4 hours every day and can be interrupted. The job uses 10 c5.4xlarge instances. What EC2 purchase option minimizes cost while meeting these requirements?
options: On-Demand instances for reliability
Spot Instances with checkpointing to resume if interrupted
1-year Reserved Instances for the 10 instances
Scheduled Reserved Instances for the 4-hour window daily
explanation: Spot Instances provide up to 90% discount over On-Demand. Since the batch job can be interrupted, Spot is ideal. Implementing checkpointing (saving progress to S3 or EFS) allows the job to resume from the last checkpoint if a Spot instance is reclaimed. This provides massive cost savings for fault-tolerant batch workloads.

question: A company has an Auto Scaling group with EC2 instances. AWS Cost Explorer shows significant data transfer costs from EC2 to the internet. The application serves images and video content. What is the best way to reduce these transfer costs?
options: Move EC2 instances to a region closer to users
Use CloudFront to cache and serve content to users
Enable Enhanced Networking to reduce transfer latency and costs
Switch to S3 static website hosting for media files
explanation: CloudFront caches content at edge locations globally. Once cached, CloudFront serves the content directly to users without fetching from EC2 each time. Data transfer from CloudFront to the internet is cheaper than EC2 to internet transfer, and cached hits eliminate origin transfer costs entirely. This is the most effective way to reduce EC2 data transfer costs for media.

question: A company runs development environments that are only used during business hours (8 AM - 6 PM, Monday to Friday). These are EC2 instances with On-Demand pricing. What is the most cost-effective change?
options: Convert to Reserved Instances for the expected 50 hours per week
Use Lambda functions instead of EC2 for development
Use AWS Instance Scheduler to automatically stop instances outside business hours
Use Spot Instances for development workloads
explanation: AWS Instance Scheduler (or a custom Lambda + EventBridge solution) automatically stops EC2 instances after business hours and starts them at the beginning of the workday. Development instances run ~50 hours/week instead of 168 hours/week, saving ~70% of EC2 costs. This is the simplest and most impactful optimization for non-production environments.

question: A company uses NAT Gateway and is seeing high data processing charges. Analysis shows most of the traffic is from EC2 instances in private subnets accessing S3 in the same region. What change eliminates this unnecessary cost?
options: Move EC2 instances to public subnets to bypass NAT Gateway
Create a VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to bypass NAT Gateway
Enable VPC Flow Logs to identify and eliminate unnecessary traffic
explanation: S3 Gateway Endpoints allow EC2 instances in private subnets to access S3 directly through the AWS network without using the NAT Gateway. S3 Gateway Endpoints are free (no per-hour or per-GB cost), and traffic to S3 through the endpoint does not incur NAT Gateway processing charges. This eliminates the unnecessary cost immediately.

question: A company wants to minimize S3 storage costs for objects that are frequently accessed for the first 30 days but then rarely accessed. The access patterns are unpredictable after 30 days. What is the most appropriate storage approach?
options: Store in S3 Standard for 30 days, then move to S3 Glacier with lifecycle rules
Store in S3 Standard and enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering after 30 days
Store in S3 Standard throughout; it's already optimized
Store in S3 Standard-IA immediately to save costs from day one
explanation: S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers based on changing access patterns without retrieval fees for objects in the Frequent or Infrequent Access tiers. After the initial 30-day period where access is certain, Intelligent-Tiering handles the unpredictable access pattern optimally, moving rarely accessed data to cheaper tiers automatically.

question: A startup has multiple microservices running on EC2. Each service handles different types of workloads with varying CPU and memory requirements. How should they purchase EC2 capacity to minimize costs while maintaining flexibility?
options: Purchase Reserved Instances for each instance type used by each microservice
Use Compute Savings Plans which apply to any EC2 instance family or size
Use Spot Instances with Spot Fleet across multiple instance types
Use On-Demand instances and apply EC2 Instance Savings Plans per service
explanation: Compute Savings Plans provide flexible discounts (up to 66%) that automatically apply to any EC2 instance usage regardless of instance family, size, AZ, region, OS, or tenancy. Unlike Reserved Instances tied to specific instance types, Compute Savings Plans work across all microservices regardless of their varying instance types, providing cost savings without purchase complexity.

question: A company is migrating from a monolithic application to microservices on AWS Lambda. They're concerned about Lambda costs for functions that run constantly. At what usage level does Lambda become more expensive than equivalent EC2?
options: Lambda is always cheaper than EC2 for any workload
When functions run more than 60% of the time, EC2 on-demand becomes cheaper
Lambda is cheaper up to approximately 1 million requests per month
When compute hours exceed a level where a dedicated EC2 instance is more economical
explanation: Lambda pricing is per request and per GB-second of compute. For functions running constantly (near 100% utilization), the per-invocation and per-GB-second costs can exceed equivalent EC2 costs. The break-even point depends on function memory, duration, and invocation rate. For very high, continuous workloads, EC2 or Fargate may be more economical than Lambda.

question: A company has a DynamoDB table with On-Demand capacity mode that handles unpredictable traffic. The table averages 10,000 RCUs and 5,000 WCUs per hour but spikes to 100,000 RCUs and 50,000 WCUs for 2 hours daily during business reports. How can they optimize costs?
options: Switch to provisioned capacity at peak levels to avoid On-Demand pricing
Keep On-Demand mode as it's already optimized for this pattern
Switch to provisioned capacity at average levels with Auto Scaling for peaks
Use DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) to reduce RCU consumption and switch to provisioned capacity
explanation: Provisioned capacity with Auto Scaling sets a baseline capacity (average) with auto-scaling to handle peaks. Provisioned capacity is cheaper than On-Demand at average load. Auto Scaling handles the 2-hour peak by scaling up and scales back down after. The combination of lower base cost with auto-scaling for predictable peaks reduces cost compared to On-Demand which prices per request regardless.

question: A company's security policy requires that all S3 objects be encrypted. They want AWS to enforce this even if developers forget to specify encryption. What is the simplest way to ensure all objects are encrypted at rest?
options: Add a bucket policy denying PutObject without encryption headers
Enable default encryption on the S3 bucket with SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS
Use Lambda to scan and encrypt objects after upload
Enforce encryption via AWS Config rules with auto-remediation
explanation: S3 default encryption automatically encrypts all new objects uploaded to the bucket using SSE-S3 (AES-256) or SSE-KMS, even if the PUT request doesn't specify encryption. This is the simplest zero-configuration approach. S3 now encrypts all objects by default, but explicitly enabling it ensures your desired key type is used.

question: A company wants to grant its mobile application users temporary access to upload files directly to S3 without routing through a backend server. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Embed IAM access keys in the mobile application code
Use Cognito Identity Pool to get temporary AWS credentials for direct S3 upload
Create pre-signed S3 URLs from a backend API for each upload
Configure S3 bucket policy allowing public write access
explanation: Pre-signed URLs are generated server-side with an expiration time and grant temporary access to a specific S3 operation (PUT) for a specific object. The mobile app gets the pre-signed URL from the backend and uploads directly to S3. No AWS credentials are exposed to the mobile app. This is more controllable than Cognito credentials since you can limit what specific object and operation is allowed per URL.

question: A company needs to implement data loss prevention (DLP) to prevent employees from downloading sensitive files stored in S3 to personal devices while allowing corporate device access. What combination of AWS services achieves this?
options: S3 bucket policies with IP range restrictions to corporate network
Macie to detect sensitive data + S3 Object Lock to prevent downloads
IAM policies using aws:RequestedRegion condition to limit access
AWS Verified Access or IAM Identity Center with device compliance conditions
explanation: AWS Verified Access (or IAM Identity Center with device trust conditions) can enforce device compliance before granting access. By integrating with endpoint management solutions (like Jamf or Intune), access can be restricted to managed/compliant corporate devices. IAM conditions can enforce specific device attributes. This provides true DLP at the access control layer.

question: A security audit found that EC2 instances in a production VPC have security groups allowing inbound SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0. The company needs to maintain SSH access for operations while eliminating this exposure. What is the recommended AWS approach?
options: Restrict SSH security group rules to the corporate office IP range
Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for shell access without inbound SSH rules
Implement a bastion host in a public subnet and restrict SSH to the bastion's SG
Use AWS VPN to create a secure tunnel for SSH access
explanation: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides secure, browser-based shell access to EC2 instances without requiring inbound port 22 or SSH keys. Instances communicate outbound to the Systems Manager service through a VPC endpoint. This eliminates SSH exposure entirely - no inbound rules needed, full audit logging in CloudTrail and Systems Manager, and session recording capability.

question: An application uses ACM (AWS Certificate Manager) for SSL/TLS certificates on an Application Load Balancer. The security team wants to ensure that only TLS 1.2 and higher is used and weak cipher suites are disabled. How should this be configured?
options: Configure the security policy on the ALB listener to use ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06 or similar
Modify the ACM certificate to specify allowed TLS versions
Use CloudFront in front of the ALB with custom SSL settings
Configure EC2 instances to only accept TLS 1.2+ in the application code
explanation: ALB security policies control the SSL/TLS protocols and cipher suites accepted by the load balancer. AWS provides predefined security policies with different combinations of protocols and cipher suites. Selecting a policy that enforces TLS 1.2+ (like ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06) ensures weak protocols and ciphers are rejected at the load balancer level.

question: A company's CloudTrail logs are stored in S3. A security engineer needs to verify that the logs haven't been tampered with. Which CloudTrail feature enables this?
options: CloudTrail log encryption with KMS
CloudTrail log file validation using SHA-256 hash digests
S3 versioning to track changes to log files
S3 Object Lock to prevent log modification
explanation: CloudTrail log file validation creates a digitally signed digest file every hour that contains SHA-256 hashes of each log file. To verify log integrity, you use the AWS CLI 'aws cloudtrail validate-logs' command, which verifies the hash chain to detect any modifications, deletions, or unauthorized changes to log files.

question: A company is using AWS Config to monitor for compliance. An AWS Config rule fires when a security group allows unrestricted inbound access on port 3389 (RDP). What is needed to automatically remediate this violation?
options: Create a CloudWatch Events rule to trigger Lambda for remediation
Configure an AWS Config remediation action with an SSM Automation document
Set up a GuardDuty finding that triggers a Lambda function
Use Security Hub with custom actions to trigger remediation Lambda
explanation: AWS Config supports direct remediation configurations. You attach a remediation action to a Config rule using an AWS Systems Manager Automation document (runbook). When the rule detects non-compliance, Config can automatically trigger the runbook to fix the issue (like removing the problematic security group rule). This is native Config auto-remediation without additional services.

question: A company processes payment card data and must comply with PCI DSS. They need to minimize the scope of PCI DSS compliance. Which architecture reduces the cardholder data environment (CDE) scope?
options: Store all payment data encrypted with KMS in a dedicated VPC
Use a payment tokenization service where only a payment provider handles actual card data
Deploy a WAF to protect all payment-related endpoints
Encrypt payment data in transit with TLS 1.3
explanation: Tokenization removes card data from the company's systems entirely. The payment provider handles actual card numbers and returns a token (non-sensitive reference). The company's systems only store and process tokens, dramatically reducing PCI DSS scope since the actual cardholder data never enters the company's environment. This is the most effective scope reduction strategy.

question: A company wants to track all changes to AWS resources (EC2, S3, RDS, IAM) and view the configuration history of any resource at any point in time. Which service provides this capability?
options: AWS CloudTrail with data events enabled
Amazon Inspector with enhanced security assessment
AWS Config with configuration recorder enabled
AWS Systems Manager Inventory
explanation: AWS Config continuously records the configuration of AWS resources and maintains a configuration history. You can view the configuration of any resource at any point in time, see what changed, and who changed it. Config provides a timeline view of resource configurations, which is different from CloudTrail which records API calls (actions) rather than configuration states.

question: A company stores files in S3 and wants to prevent accidental deletion of important files while still allowing updates to existing files. What S3 feature should be enabled?
options: S3 Object Lock in GOVERNANCE mode
S3 MFA Delete combined with versioning
S3 versioning alone
S3 Replication to a backup bucket
explanation: S3 MFA Delete requires multi-factor authentication to delete a specific object version or change versioning state. With versioning enabled, overwriting a file creates a new version (not a destructive operation). MFA Delete ensures that even if credentials are compromised, an attacker cannot permanently delete objects without the MFA device. Versioning alone allows deletion without MFA.

question: A company has an application that writes data to S3 from multiple EC2 instances. Another application reads the latest data. Previously S3 had eventual consistency for overwrite PUTs which caused stale reads. What is the current behavior?
options: S3 still has eventual consistency for overwrite PUTs and DELETEs
S3 now provides strong read-after-write consistency for all operations including PUTs, DELETEs, and LIST
S3 provides strong consistency only for new object PUTs, not overwrites
S3 consistency depends on the storage class used
explanation: Since December 2020, Amazon S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency for all S3 GET, PUT, LIST, and DELETE operations. This means immediately after a PUT or DELETE operation, subsequent GET and LIST operations will reflect the change. The old eventual consistency behavior no longer applies to any S3 operations.

question: A company needs to process 1 million images daily. Each image processing job is independent and takes approximately 5 minutes. The jobs need to complete within 8 hours. Which AWS service combination is most cost-effective?
options: EC2 Auto Scaling group with target tracking on queue depth
AWS Batch with Spot Instances for compute
Lambda with SQS triggering (Lambda handles images up to 15 minute timeout)
Step Functions orchestrating ECS tasks with Fargate Spot
explanation: AWS Batch is purpose-built for batch processing workloads. It automatically provisions and manages compute resources, supports Spot Instances (up to 90% savings), and optimally bins jobs to minimize cost. For 5-minute jobs with a 8-hour SLA for 1M images, Batch automatically scales out compute, uses Spot to minimize cost, and manages the job queue. It's the most cost-effective for this use case.

question: An application architecture uses SNS to fan out to multiple SQS queues. One queue needs to only receive messages about 'order.created' events and another queue needs to receive 'order.shipped' and 'order.delivered' events. How should this be implemented?
options: Create separate SNS topics for each event type
Use SNS message filtering with subscription filter policies on each SQS subscription
Use Lambda to route messages from a single SQS queue to other queues by event type
Use EventBridge instead of SNS with rules matching specific event patterns
explanation: SNS subscription filter policies allow each SQS subscription to specify which messages it wants to receive based on message attributes. The publisher sends all events to a single SNS topic with message attributes (like event_type), and each SQS subscriber only receives events matching its filter policy. This is more cost-effective than multiple topics and more scalable than Lambda routing.

question: A company uses RDS MySQL and needs to migrate to Aurora MySQL. They need zero downtime migration with minimal performance impact during migration. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with ongoing replication
Create an Aurora Read Replica from the RDS MySQL instance and promote it
Take an RDS snapshot and restore to Aurora
Use mysqldump to export and import data to Aurora
explanation: Creating an Aurora MySQL Read Replica from an existing RDS MySQL instance performs the migration with minimal downtime. The replica stays in sync via replication. When ready to cut over, you stop writes to RDS, wait for the replica to catch up, then promote the Aurora replica to a standalone Aurora cluster. This minimizes downtime to just the cutover window.

question: A company uses Kinesis Data Streams to process clickstream data. A Lambda function is triggered by the stream. The team finds that Lambda invocations are behind by 4 hours during peak traffic, and the Kinesis stream is dropping records. What should be done?
options: Increase Lambda memory and timeout settings
Add more shards to the Kinesis stream to increase throughput
Enable Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out for the Lambda consumer
Switch to Kinesis Data Firehose which automatically scales
explanation: Kinesis Data Streams throughput scales with the number of shards (1MB/s write, 2MB/s read per shard). If the stream is dropping records and Lambda is behind, the stream doesn't have enough capacity. Adding more shards increases the stream's total throughput. Enhanced Fan-Out helps with multiple consumers competing for bandwidth but doesn't help if the stream itself is at capacity.

question: A company's application uses both EC2 instances and Lambda functions. They want to share code between Lambda functions including a 250MB dependency package. What is the most appropriate solution?
options: Package the dependency in each Lambda function's deployment package
Use Lambda Layers to share the common dependency across multiple functions
Store the dependency in EFS and mount it to Lambda functions
Use ECR container images which can be up to 10GB for Lambda
explanation: Lambda Layers allow you to package shared code, libraries, and dependencies separately from function code. Multiple Lambda functions can reference the same layer, and the layer is cached in the execution environment. Layers can be up to 50MB compressed (250MB uncompressed), are versioned, and can be shared across accounts. This is specifically designed for this use case.

question: A company needs to implement an ETL pipeline that reads from S3, transforms data using SQL, and writes to Redshift daily. The pipeline should run without managing servers. What is the most appropriate service?
options: EMR cluster with Spark for transformation and JDBC to Redshift
AWS Glue ETL job with serverless Spark execution
Lambda function with pandas for transformation
AWS Data Pipeline with EC2 resources
explanation: AWS Glue is a fully managed, serverless ETL service. Glue ETL jobs run on managed Apache Spark infrastructure with no servers to provision or manage. Glue has native connectors to S3 and Redshift, supports SQL transformations via Glue Studio, and can be scheduled as needed. The Data Catalog integrates schema discovery with both source and destination.

question: A web application uses ElastiCache Redis for session management. Users report being logged out intermittently. The team identifies the cause as ElastiCache node failures causing session data loss. What is the correct configuration to prevent this?
options: Enable ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ with automatic failover
Use ElastiCache Memcached instead of Redis for better reliability
Increase ElastiCache node type to reduce memory pressure
Enable ElastiCache Redis cluster mode with multiple shards
explanation: ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ with automatic failover maintains a primary node and one or more replica nodes. When the primary fails, ElastiCache automatically promotes a replica to primary. Since Redis replicates data to replicas, session data is preserved during failover. The automatic failover typically completes within a few minutes with minimal data loss.

question: A startup runs a mobile backend with API Gateway and Lambda. During a product launch, they expect traffic to spike 100x for one hour. Lambda has a default concurrency limit. What should they do before the launch?
options: Switch to EC2 Auto Scaling for the launch period
Request a concurrency limit increase from AWS and configure reserved concurrency for critical functions
Enable API Gateway caching to reduce Lambda invocations
Scale up Lambda function memory to handle more concurrent requests
explanation: Lambda has account-level concurrency limits (default 1,000 in most regions). For a 100x traffic spike, you should proactively request a concurrency limit increase from AWS Support before the launch. Additionally, set reserved concurrency for critical functions to guarantee they get capacity. Reserved concurrency also prevents one function from consuming all account concurrency.

question: A company's microservices architecture uses Lambda. One Lambda function calls another Lambda function and waits for the response before proceeding. The downstream Lambda sometimes takes 10 minutes. What issue does this create and what is the solution?
options: Lambda timeout (15 min max) - increase calling Lambda memory to improve performance
Lambda timeout - restructure to use async invocation with SQS and polling
Lambda cost - use Step Functions to orchestrate instead of Lambda-to-Lambda calls
No issue - Lambda-to-Lambda synchronous calls support 15-minute timeout
explanation: Synchronous Lambda-to-Lambda calls (RequestResponse) create a chain where both functions' timeouts are consumed. More importantly, you pay for both Lambdas waiting (idle time is billed). AWS Step Functions orchestrate workflows where each step's timeout is managed independently, steps run sequentially or in parallel without idle Lambda waiting costs, and the execution history is maintained for debugging.

question: A company uses Route 53 with weighted routing to test a new application version. They want to send 10% of traffic to version 2 and 90% to version 1 without any end user impact. What Route 53 configuration achieves this?
options: Create two A records with weights 10 and 90 pointing to ALBs for each version
Use Route 53 latency-based routing and deploy version 2 in a region closer to 10% of users
Use geolocation routing to send 10% of countries to version 2
Configure Route 53 traffic flow with a weighted rule pointing 10% to version 2
explanation: Route 53 weighted routing allows you to associate multiple records with the same DNS name and assign relative weights. Records with weight 10 and 90 will distribute traffic approximately 10%/90% between the two ALBs (or other endpoints). This is the classic A/B testing or canary deployment pattern for gradual traffic shifting.

question: A company's application stores data in DynamoDB and has a requirement to keep a 90-day changelog of all item modifications for audit purposes. What is the best approach?
options: Enable DynamoDB Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) and query the backup history
Use DynamoDB Streams with a Lambda function to write change records to a separate audit table
Enable DynamoDB Streams and deliver to Kinesis Firehose to S3 for 90-day storage
Use CloudTrail data events for DynamoDB to capture all API calls
explanation: DynamoDB Streams captures item-level changes (INSERT, MODIFY, REMOVE) with before and after images. Kinesis Data Firehose can consume the stream and deliver records to S3 with configurable retention. S3 lifecycle rules can keep data for 90 days. This is efficient, cost-effective, and doesn't require managing a separate audit DynamoDB table which would incur additional storage and write costs.

question: An application runs on EC2 and stores session data in a local file. When the Auto Scaling group scales in (terminates instances), users lose their sessions. What architectural change fixes this?
options: Enable ASG instance termination protection for active session instances
Use sticky sessions on the ALB to route users to the same instance
Move session data to ElastiCache or DynamoDB (externalize session state)
Implement connection draining on the ALB to complete active sessions before termination
explanation: The correct approach is to externalize session state to a distributed store like ElastiCache (Redis/Memcached) or DynamoDB. When any EC2 instance can serve any user's request (since session data is in a shared store), the Auto Scaling group can freely scale in and out without impacting user sessions. This enables true stateless architecture for the EC2 tier.

question: A company needs to run a critical workload that cannot be interrupted. They want the lowest cost EC2 option that guarantees instance availability. Which option is most appropriate?
options: Spot Instances with high bid price
On-Demand Instances
Reserved Instances (Standard, 1-year, no upfront)
Dedicated Hosts with Reserved pricing
explanation: Reserved Instances guarantee capacity (when you use capacity reservations) and provide significant savings (up to 40% for 1-year, no upfront over On-Demand). They cannot be interrupted like Spot Instances. For workloads that cannot be interrupted and run continuously, Reserved Instances provide guaranteed availability at lower cost than On-Demand while Spot Instances have 2-minute interruption notices.

question: A company processes genomic data in batches using a cluster of EC2 instances. Jobs run for 6-12 hours and can checkpoint progress to S3. The computation is not time-critical. What EC2 strategy minimizes cost?
options: On-Demand instances with Auto Scaling based on queue depth
Spot instances using Spot Fleet with diversified instance types and checkpointing
Reserved instances for predictable baseline with On-Demand for overflow
AWS Fargate with Spot pricing for containerized batch jobs
explanation: Spot Fleet with diversified instance types requests capacity from multiple instance pools, reducing the chance that all instances are reclaimed simultaneously. Checkpointing to S3 allows jobs to resume from the last checkpoint if interrupted. This provides up to 90% savings over On-Demand. The diversified strategy and checkpointing together make Spot viable even for 6-12 hour jobs.

question: A company transfers 100TB of data monthly between EC2 in US-East-1 and an on-premises data center. Data transfer costs are very high. What is the most cost-effective solution to reduce these costs?
options: Use AWS Direct Connect instead of internet for data transfers
Use AWS Snowball for monthly data transfers instead of network transfer
Compress all data before transfer to reduce bandwidth
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration for faster but cheaper transfers
explanation: AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated network connection between on-premises and AWS. Data transferred over Direct Connect is cheaper than internet data transfer rates. For 100TB/month of consistent data transfer, the Direct Connect monthly port fee combined with lower data transfer pricing provides significant savings over internet-based transfer.

question: A company's RDS Aurora cluster has one writer and 5 reader instances. The engineering team notices that all 5 readers are idle most of the time. What change optimizes cost while maintaining read availability?
options: Remove all reader instances and use only the writer
Enable Aurora Auto Scaling to automatically add/remove reader instances based on CPU/connections
Switch to Aurora Serverless v2 which scales readers to zero when idle
Reduce reader instance types to the smallest available
explanation: Aurora Auto Scaling adds reader instances when read traffic increases and removes them when it decreases. Setting the minimum to 1 (for availability) and maximum to 5 allows the cluster to right-size automatically. This eliminates the cost of idle reader instances while maintaining the ability to scale up for read spikes. Aurora Serverless v2 could also work but changes the deployment model significantly.

question: A company uses AWS to host a marketing website that receives most traffic Monday-Friday during business hours. They're using On-Demand EC2 with Auto Scaling. How can they reduce costs while maintaining performance?
options: Use Scheduled Actions in Auto Scaling to scale down outside business hours
Switch all instances to Reserved Instances for the lowest cost
Use Spot Instances which are cheapest during off-peak hours
Implement a CDN to cache content and reduce required EC2 capacity
explanation: Scheduled Actions in Auto Scaling allow you to set minimum/maximum/desired capacity at specific times. Configure high capacity during business hours (Mon-Fri, 8-6 PM) and low capacity otherwise. Since the traffic pattern is predictable, scheduled scaling proactively adjusts capacity. Combined with a CDN for caching static content, this significantly reduces EC2 costs.

question: A company uses S3 to store application logs. The access patterns show: 90% of queries request logs from the last 7 days, 8% request logs from the past month, and 2% are older. The old logs must be retrievable within 24 hours. What lifecycle configuration minimizes cost?
options: Keep recent in S3 Standard, move 7-day-old logs to S3-IA, move 30-day-old to Glacier
Keep all logs in S3 Standard-IA since most access is within 30 days
Keep in S3 Standard for 7 days, move to S3-IA for 23 days, move to Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 30 days
Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for automatic optimization
explanation: The access pattern supports: S3 Standard for the first 7 days (high access), S3-IA for days 7-30 (moderate access, 8%), Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 30 days (2% access, 24-hour retrieval meets the SLA since Flexible Retrieval offers 3-5 hours standard or 12 hours bulk retrieval, well within 24 hours). This lifecycle matches storage costs to actual access patterns.

question: A startup is building a new SaaS application. They expect low traffic initially (< 100 requests/day) growing to millions of requests/day over 2 years. Which compute architecture is most cost-effective during initial low-traffic phase while scaling for growth?
options: Start with t3.small EC2 instance and upgrade as needed
Use serverless (Lambda + API Gateway) from the start; scales to zero cost at low traffic
Use ECS Fargate with minimum task count of 1
Purchase 1-year Reserved Instances for the expected future capacity
explanation: Lambda + API Gateway is ideal for a startup in early stages. At <100 requests/day, Lambda costs are essentially zero (AWS Free Tier includes 1M requests/month). As traffic grows, Lambda scales automatically without architecture changes, and you pay only for what you use. There's no cost during zero traffic. This allows the startup to conserve capital while building a scalable foundation.

question: A company needs to store large media files (average 2GB) for long-term archival. The files need to be retrieved within 5 minutes when requested. Which S3 storage class and retrieval option meets this requirement at the lowest cost?
options: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval with Expedited retrieval
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval for millisecond access
S3 One Zone-IA for lowest per-GB cost
S3 Standard-IA with provisioned capacity
explanation: S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval is specifically designed for archive data that needs millisecond retrieval. It provides the same retrieval speed as S3 Standard but at much lower storage cost than S3 Standard-IA. Since retrieval needs to be 'within 5 minutes', both Instant Retrieval and Expedited Flexible Retrieval work, but Glacier Instant Retrieval is designed for this use case at lower storage cost.

question: A company runs a web application with an Application Load Balancer in front of an EC2 Auto Scaling group. The security team wants to block traffic from specific countries. What is the simplest solution?
options: Add NACLs to block IP ranges from specific countries
Use CloudFront with geographic restrictions in front of the ALB
Configure WAF with geographic match conditions on the ALB
Use Route 53 geolocation routing to block specific countries
explanation: AWS WAF Web ACL with geographic match conditions can be attached directly to an Application Load Balancer. The geo match rule can block requests from specified countries (using ISO country codes). This is simpler than CloudFront (which adds another layer) and more maintainable than IP range-based NACLs which change frequently.

question: A company runs a three-tier web application (web, app, database). They want to implement auto-healing at all tiers. The web tier is stateless, the app tier maintains local state, and the database is RDS MySQL. What combination provides auto-healing at all tiers?
options: ASG for web tier, ASG for app tier with sticky sessions, RDS Multi-AZ
ASG for web tier, ASG for app tier with external session store, RDS Multi-AZ with automated backups
CloudFront for web, ASG for app, RDS Read Replicas for database
ELB for web, dedicated EC2 for app, RDS Multi-AZ
explanation: Auto Scaling Groups (ASG) replace failed instances automatically for web and app tiers. The app tier's local state must be externalized (to ElastiCache or DynamoDB) to enable ASG to replace instances without state loss. RDS Multi-AZ provides automatic failover for the database tier. This combination enables true auto-healing at all tiers.

question: A company needs to implement a data lake on AWS. They have structured data from RDS, semi-structured data from DynamoDB, and unstructured data from S3. They need a unified catalog for all data that enables SQL queries. What is the recommended architecture?
options: Migrate all data to Redshift for unified querying
Use AWS Glue Data Catalog with Athena for unified SQL queries across all data sources
Use EMR with Hive metastore as the unified catalog
Use RDS PostgreSQL as the centralized store for all data types
explanation: AWS Glue Data Catalog serves as a unified metadata repository for structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data. Glue crawlers automatically discover and catalog schemas. Amazon Athena can query data in S3 (structured/unstructured) using Glue catalog, and has connectors for DynamoDB and RDS, enabling federated queries across all data sources without moving data.

question: A company's mobile app users upload photos to S3. They want to automatically generate thumbnails when photos are uploaded. The solution must be scalable and cost-effective. What is the best architecture?
options: EC2 Auto Scaling group polling S3 for new uploads
S3 Event Notifications triggering Lambda to generate thumbnails
SQS queue fed by S3 events with EC2 worker instances processing
EventBridge with S3 rule triggering Fargate task for processing
explanation: S3 Event Notifications can trigger Lambda directly when objects are uploaded (s3:ObjectCreated:* events). Lambda is invoked per upload, generates the thumbnail, and saves to a separate S3 location. This is fully serverless, scales automatically to handle any number of concurrent uploads, and costs nothing when there are no uploads. This is the classic and recommended AWS serverless pattern for this use case.

question: A company runs a microservices application where Service A calls Service B synchronously over HTTP. Service B is experiencing latency spikes causing timeouts in Service A. What pattern should be implemented to make Service A resilient to Service B's latency?
options: Increase Service A's HTTP timeout to 60 seconds
Implement a circuit breaker pattern in Service A
Deploy Service B in multiple AZs to reduce latency
Use an Application Load Balancer between Service A and Service B
explanation: A circuit breaker prevents cascading failures. When Service B starts failing or timing out, the circuit breaker 'opens' and Service A immediately returns a fallback response instead of waiting for Service B. After a cooldown period, it tries again ('half-open'). This pattern prevents Service A from being slowed down by Service B's latency issues and avoids resource exhaustion from blocked threads.

question: A company wants to decouple their order processing system. When an order is placed, 5 different services need to process the order: inventory, payment, shipping, notification, and analytics. Each service needs to receive every order. What is the best architecture?
options: Use a single SQS queue; each consumer reads all messages and filters by service type
Use SNS topic fan-out to 5 separate SQS queues, one per service
Use EventBridge with 5 rules sending to 5 different Lambda functions
Use Kinesis Data Streams with 5 separate consumer applications
explanation: SNS fan-out pattern sends each order to one SNS topic which delivers copies to all 5 SQS queues simultaneously. Each service independently reads from its own queue at its own pace. This fully decouples the services - if analytics is slow or down, it doesn't affect inventory or payment processing. Each queue can have different visibility timeouts, DLQs, and scaling configurations.

question: A company uses CloudFront to serve their website. They want to serve different content to users based on their device type (mobile, tablet, desktop) without maintaining separate origins. What CloudFront feature enables this?
options: Configure CloudFront geographic restrictions by device type
Use CloudFront Lambda@Edge to detect device type and serve appropriate content
Configure CloudFront cache behaviors with different origins per device
Use CloudFront origin groups with multiple origins
explanation: Lambda@Edge runs custom Lambda code at CloudFront edge locations on every request. By inspecting the CloudFront-Is-Mobile-Viewer, CloudFront-Is-Tablet-Viewer headers (automatically set by CloudFront), Lambda@Edge can modify the request or response to serve device-appropriate content from the same origin, or route to different origins, without changing the URL structure.

question: A company stores scientific research data in S3 that must be retained for 10 years with no possibility of deletion or modification, even by administrators. Compliance requires proof that the data hasn't been altered. Which combination provides this?
options: S3 versioning + S3 lifecycle rules to transition older versions
S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode with WORM protection for 10 years + CloudTrail
S3 MFA Delete + S3 bucket policy denying delete operations
S3 Glacier Vault Lock with compliance policy for 10 years
explanation: S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode prevents object versions from being overwritten or deleted by any user, including the root account, for the specified retention period. WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection is guaranteed. Combined with CloudTrail auditing access and S3 server-side checksum validation, this provides immutable storage with cryptographic proof of integrity for compliance.

question: A company needs to provide secure remote access to their AWS resources for employees working from home. The solution should not require an always-on VPN connection. What AWS service provides browser-based, zero-trust access to internal AWS applications?
options: AWS Client VPN for remote access
AWS Site-to-Site VPN with split tunneling
AWS Verified Access for zero-trust application access
AWS Direct Connect for corporate connectivity
explanation: AWS Verified Access provides secure access to internal applications without a VPN. It validates each request against trust providers (identity, device posture) and grants access on a per-request basis based on policies. Users access applications through their browser without needing VPN clients or always-on tunnels, implementing zero-trust network access (ZTNA) principles.

question: A company deploys a microservices application using Amazon ECS with EC2 launch type. They want to ensure even distribution of tasks across EC2 instances and automatically place tasks on the instance with the least memory usage. Which ECS task placement strategy achieves this?
options: binpack placement strategy based on memory
spread placement strategy across instances
random placement strategy
Fargate launch type handles placement automatically
explanation: The 'binpack' task placement strategy places tasks on the EC2 instance with the least available memory (or CPU). This packs tasks densely onto instances, maximizing utilization and freeing up other instances which can then be terminated by Auto Scaling, reducing cost. The 'spread' strategy distributes evenly for high availability, while 'binpack' optimizes resource utilization.

question: A company hosts a stateful application on EC2. During load testing, they notice that when new instances are added by Auto Scaling, they take 10 minutes to warm up and aren't ready to serve traffic. During this time, the ALB sends traffic to the new instances causing errors. What should be configured?
options: Increase ALB health check intervals during scaling events
Configure Auto Scaling warm-up settings and set the ALB target group's slow start mode
Use lifecycle hooks to delay the instance entering the InService state
Increase the ALB deregistration delay (connection draining) setting
explanation: ALB target group's slow start mode gradually increases traffic to new targets over a configurable period (30-900 seconds), allowing the application to warm up before receiving full traffic. Auto Scaling warm-up settings (instance warmup period) tell Auto Scaling to wait before considering the new instance in scaling metrics, preventing further scaling actions during warm-up.

question: A company wants to migrate 500TB of data from their on-premises NFS storage to Amazon EFS. The migration must complete within 2 weeks and minimize impact on on-premises systems. What is the recommended service?
options: AWS DataSync for automated data transfer from NFS to EFS
AWS Snowball to physically transport data to AWS
AWS Storage Gateway File Gateway to replicate data to EFS
rsync over Direct Connect to transfer data directly to EFS
explanation: AWS DataSync is a fully managed data transfer service optimized for migrating NFS and SMB data to AWS. It can transfer data 10x faster than open-source tools, automatically handles scheduling, monitoring, error detection, and retries. DataSync uses parallel multi-part transfers and compresses data, making 500TB in 2 weeks feasible. It connects over the internet or Direct Connect.

question: A company runs a news website where most content is read but rarely updated. The website serves 10 million daily visitors globally. Page load times are critical. Currently, pages are dynamically generated from a CMS and take 500ms to render. What is the best optimization?
options: Use ElastiCache Redis to cache rendered pages for 1 hour
Use CloudFront with cache-control headers to cache rendered pages at edge
Migrate to Aurora Global Database for lower read latency
Use EC2 C5 instances for faster page rendering
explanation: CloudFront caches rendered HTML pages at edge locations globally. Most visitors get cached responses from the nearest edge location (< 50ms) instead of the 500ms origin render time. Cache-control headers can set appropriate TTLs. Only cache misses or invalidated content hits the origin. This provides massive performance improvement and dramatically reduces origin server load.

question: A company wants to implement a message queue that ensures messages are processed in the exact order they are sent, with no duplicate processing, and needs to send 5,000 messages per second. Which SQS configuration should be used?
options: SQS Standard queue with consumer-side deduplication logic
SQS FIFO queue in standard mode (no batching) - 3,000 TPS limit
SQS FIFO queue with message batching enabled - up to 30,000 TPS
SQS Standard queue with message sequence numbers in the body
explanation: SQS FIFO queues guarantee ordering and exactly-once processing. With batching enabled (SendMessageBatch API, up to 10 messages per batch), FIFO queues support up to 30,000 messages per second (3,000 API calls/second × 10 messages/batch). At 5,000 messages/second, FIFO with batching meets both the ordering and throughput requirements.

question: A company's Lambda function processes events from an SQS queue. During peak hours, the queue depth grows to millions of messages and Lambda costs spike. What change maintains processing while reducing costs?
options: Reduce Lambda memory allocation to minimum required
Use Lambda reserved concurrency to limit maximum concurrent executions
Use SQS batch processing with Lambda to process up to 10 messages per invocation
Switch the Lambda trigger to poll less frequently
explanation: Lambda SQS batch processing (batch size 1-10,000 for standard, 1-10 per message group for FIFO) invokes Lambda once with multiple messages instead of once per message. Processing 10 messages per invocation reduces Lambda invocations (and their overhead) by 10x. With batch windows, Lambda can also wait to accumulate more messages, further reducing invocations and costs.

question: A company needs to build a search feature for their e-commerce platform that supports full-text search, fuzzy matching, and faceted filtering across 10 million product listings. What AWS service should be used?
options: Amazon RDS MySQL with FULLTEXT indexes
Amazon DynamoDB with GSIs for each search attribute
Amazon OpenSearch Service (formerly Elasticsearch Service)
Amazon Kendra for intelligent document search
explanation: Amazon OpenSearch Service is designed for full-text search workloads. It supports full-text search with relevance scoring, fuzzy matching for typo tolerance, faceted search for filtering (category, price range, brand), and can scale to handle millions of documents. RDS FULLTEXT is limited in functionality and scale. DynamoDB doesn't support full-text search natively.

question: A company uses AWS CodePipeline to deploy to production. The pipeline stages are: Source (CodeCommit) → Build (CodeBuild) → Test → Deploy (CodeDeploy on EC2). If the production deployment fails, they want the previous version to be automatically restored with minimal downtime. What CodeDeploy configuration supports this?
options: Blue/Green deployment with automatic rollback on failure
In-place deployment with automatic rollback on CloudWatch alarm
Rolling deployment with half at a time and rollback policy
Canary deployment with 10% initial rollout and automatic full rollback
explanation: CodeDeploy Blue/Green deployment provisions a new set of instances (Green) alongside the existing production instances (Blue). After testing, traffic is shifted from Blue to Green. If the deployment fails or alarms fire, CodeDeploy can automatically roll back by shifting traffic back to the Blue instances (which are still running). This provides the fastest rollback with zero downtime.

question: A company has multiple AWS accounts and teams. They want a central place to manage users and allow them to access different AWS accounts with different permission sets using single sign-on. What is the recommended solution?
options: Create IAM users in each account and share credentials across accounts
Use IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) with AWS Organizations
Implement federation with each account's IAM SAML provider separately
Use IAM cross-account roles and require users to switch roles manually
explanation: AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) provides centralized identity management for multiple AWS accounts. Users sign in once and can access all their permitted accounts and roles through a single portal. It integrates with external identity providers (Active Directory, Okta), manages permission sets applied across accounts, and provides an audit trail of all access through CloudTrail.

question: A company's application makes database calls in a loop, resulting in 1000 DynamoDB read operations per request. Performance is poor and costs are high. The data read is mostly static reference data. What is the best optimization?
options: Switch to DynamoDB batch operations to reduce API calls
Implement DAX as a caching layer to serve repeated reads from memory
Move static data to ElastiCache and query there instead
Use DynamoDB parallel scans to improve throughput
explanation: DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) is an in-memory cache with DynamoDB-compatible API. Static reference data read repeatedly will be served from DAX's cache on subsequent requests, with microsecond latency vs. single-digit millisecond for DynamoDB. DAX reduces both latency and DynamoDB RCU consumption significantly. For application code, DAX is a drop-in replacement using the same DynamoDB calls.

question: An application writes millions of small files (1-10 KB) to EFS. Performance is poor - writes are taking much longer than expected. What EFS performance configuration should be applied?
options: Switch from General Purpose to Max I/O performance mode
Enable EFS Bursting Throughput mode with adequate burst credits
Switch from Bursting to Provisioned Throughput mode
Use EFS Infrequent Access storage class for cost savings
explanation: EFS Bursting Throughput provides throughput that scales with storage size. For small files, you might not have enough stored data to earn adequate burst credits. Provisioned Throughput allows you to specify the throughput level you need (in MB/s) regardless of storage size. This guarantees consistent throughput for write-heavy small file workloads independent of the amount of data stored.

question: A company deploys a containerized application with Amazon ECS on Fargate. The application needs to access an RDS MySQL database. The connection string including the password is currently set as a Fargate task environment variable. What is the secure alternative?
options: Encrypt the password with KMS and store in environment variable
Store the connection string in Secrets Manager and reference it as a secret in the ECS task definition
Use ECS task role to access RDS without a password
Store the connection string in EFS and mount it to the Fargate task
explanation: ECS task definitions support referencing secrets from AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store directly. The secret is injected as an environment variable at task launch time, not stored in the task definition itself. The task role needs permission to access the secret, and the secret value is never visible in the task definition. Secrets Manager also supports automatic rotation.

question: A company needs an outbound internet connection for their EC2 instances in a private subnet. They want the connection to be highly available without managing NAT EC2 instances. What AWS-managed service should be used?
options: Egress-only internet gateway (for IPv6)
NAT Gateway deployed in one AZ with Elastic IP
NAT Gateway deployed in each AZ for high availability
Internet Gateway with security group restrictions
explanation: For high availability, deploy a NAT Gateway in each AZ and update each private subnet's route table to use the NAT Gateway in the same AZ. If one AZ's NAT Gateway fails, instances in other AZs are unaffected because they use their own AZ's NAT Gateway. A single NAT Gateway is a single point of failure for all private subnets using it.

question: A company's DynamoDB table receives inconsistent write patterns: up to 1 million WCUs per second for 5 minutes during a daily batch load, then very low traffic for the rest of the day. What is the most cost-effective DynamoDB capacity configuration?
options: Provisioned capacity at 1 million WCUs with auto-scaling configured
On-Demand capacity mode which handles any traffic level automatically
Provisioned capacity at average WCUs with manual adjustment before batch
Scheduled auto-scaling to increase capacity before batch and decrease after
explanation: Scheduled auto-scaling allows you to programmatically adjust DynamoDB provisioned capacity at specific times. Set high capacity before the batch window and scale back down after. This is more cost-effective than On-Demand mode (which charges per request at a higher rate) or always-on high provisioned capacity. It requires knowing when the batch runs, which the question implies (daily).

question: An application uses API Gateway and Lambda for a public REST API. The team notices high costs because Lambda is frequently invoked for identical read requests to the same resources. What optimization should be implemented?
options: Enable API Gateway caching on the stage with appropriate cache keys
Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency to reduce cold start costs
Implement client-side caching in the mobile application
Switch from REST API to HTTP API which costs less
explanation: API Gateway stage-level caching caches responses for specified TTL periods. Subsequent identical requests return the cached response without invoking Lambda. The cache key is configurable (path, query parameters, headers). For read-heavy APIs with repetitive requests, this significantly reduces Lambda invocations and latency while adding a small cache cost that's typically much lower than the Lambda cost savings.

question: A company runs an application in a VPC and needs to connect to a partner company's application running in their VPC. Both companies use non-overlapping CIDR ranges. The connection should be private (no internet) and highly available. What AWS service should be used?
options: VPC Peering between the two VPCs
AWS PrivateLink (VPC Interface Endpoint) to expose the partner service
Site-to-Site VPN between the two VPCs
AWS Transit Gateway connecting both VPCs
explanation: AWS PrivateLink allows the partner to expose their service as a VPC endpoint service. Your VPC creates an interface VPC endpoint to the partner's service. Traffic flows over the AWS backbone, never the internet. PrivateLink doesn't require peering (no route management, no CIDR conflicts), works even with overlapping CIDRs, and is the standard for SaaS-style private connectivity.

question: A company wants to analyze VPC network traffic to detect unusual patterns like port scanning, protocol anomalies, and unusual traffic volumes. Which service provides this visibility?
options: VPC Flow Logs sent to CloudWatch Logs for analysis
AWS Network Firewall with intrusion detection rules
Amazon GuardDuty which analyzes VPC Flow Logs automatically
AWS Inspector analyzing network reachability
explanation: Amazon GuardDuty continuously analyzes VPC Flow Logs (along with CloudTrail and DNS logs) using machine learning to detect threats like port scans, known malicious IP communication, unusual protocols, and unusual traffic patterns. Unlike manually analyzing Flow Logs, GuardDuty applies intelligent threat detection and provides actionable findings without requiring query infrastructure.

question: A company deploys new application versions using a canary deployment strategy. After deployment, a CloudWatch alarm detects a spike in application errors. The company wants the deployment to automatically roll back when this happens. What should be configured?
options: AWS CodeDeploy with automatic rollback trigger on CloudWatch alarm
AWS CodePipeline manual approval step with monitoring period
CloudWatch Events rule to invoke Lambda that calls CodeDeploy rollback
Elastic Beanstalk managed platform updates with health-based rollback
explanation: AWS CodeDeploy supports rollback triggers based on CloudWatch alarms. When configuring the deployment group, you can specify CloudWatch alarms that, when in ALARM state, trigger an automatic rollback of the deployment. This integrates naturally with canary deployments (where a small percentage of traffic goes to the new version) to detect errors early and roll back automatically.

question: A company uses AWS to host multiple websites. They want to route users to different domains based on the URL path: /api/* goes to a backend EC2 fleet, /static/* goes to S3, and /* goes to a WordPress EC2 instance. What AWS feature provides this routing?
options: Route 53 with multiple A records for different subdomains
Application Load Balancer with path-based routing rules
CloudFront with multiple origin configurations and cache behaviors
Network Load Balancer with target group selection by path
explanation: Application Load Balancer supports path-based routing rules. You create listener rules that match specific URL paths and route to different target groups. /api/* routes to the API target group (EC2 fleet), /static/* routes to an S3 bucket (or EC2 serving static files), and /* (default) routes to the WordPress target group. This is a core ALB feature at no additional cost.

question: A company migrates a web application from on-premises to AWS. The application uses a Microsoft SQL Server database. They want to minimize licensing costs. What deployment option minimizes SQL Server licensing costs on AWS?
options: Run SQL Server on EC2 with Bring Your Own License (BYOL)
Use RDS for SQL Server with License Included pricing
Use RDS Custom for SQL Server
Migrate to Amazon Aurora which doesn't require SQL Server license
explanation: Migrating from SQL Server to Amazon Aurora (using Schema Conversion Tool for SQL migration) eliminates SQL Server licensing costs entirely. Aurora is open-source compatible (MySQL or PostgreSQL) with no vendor licensing. While this requires migration effort, it's the most cost-effective long-term approach. AWS SCT can help convert SQL Server-specific syntax.

question: A company's DevOps team uses EC2 instances as build servers. These instances are idle 70% of the time. Which service eliminates the need for persistent build server instances?
options: AWS CodeBuild which provides fully managed, ephemeral build environments
EC2 Spot Instances for idle-time savings
EC2 Auto Scaling to terminate idle instances
AWS Fargate for containerized build environments
explanation: AWS CodeBuild is a fully managed continuous integration service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces artifacts. Build environments are provisioned only when a build runs and terminated afterward - no persistent servers. You pay only for the build minutes used, eliminating the 70% idle time cost. CodeBuild integrates with CodePipeline, GitHub, and Bitbucket.

question: A company needs to implement a multi-account strategy for their growing AWS usage. They have separate teams for production, development, and security. What AWS service should be used as the foundation for this strategy?
options: IAM Identity Center for centralized access management across accounts
AWS Organizations for central governance and policy management
AWS Control Tower for automated multi-account environment setup
AWS Resource Access Manager for cross-account resource sharing
explanation: AWS Control Tower provides the easiest way to set up and govern a secure, compliant multi-account AWS environment. It builds on AWS Organizations and sets up a landing zone with account vending (Account Factory), pre-configured guardrails (SCPs + Config rules), a log archive account, an audit account, and IAM Identity Center. It's the recommended starting point for organizations building multi-account AWS environments.

question: A company stores sensitive patient health information (PHI) in RDS. The compliance team requires that any database backup exported to S3 must be encrypted and the key must be controlled by the company. What configuration meets this requirement?
options: Enable RDS automated backups which are encrypted by default
Export RDS snapshots to S3 and enable S3 default encryption with SSE-S3
Export RDS snapshots to S3 with SSE-KMS using a customer managed key
Use RDS database activity streams to monitor and control backup access
explanation: When exporting RDS snapshots to S3, you can specify a KMS key for encryption. Using a customer managed key (CMK) gives the company full control over the key policy, can revoke access, and has complete audit logs of key usage. SSE-S3 uses AWS managed keys which don't provide customer control. This meets HIPAA requirements for customer-controlled encryption keys.

question: A company has a legacy on-premises application that uses SFTP to receive files from partners. They're migrating to AWS and want to maintain SFTP compatibility for partners without them needing to change anything. What AWS service supports this?
options: AWS Transfer Family (SFTP) with S3 as the backend storage
AWS DataSync with SFTP endpoint for partner connectivity
EC2 instance running an SFTP server with EFS storage
S3 with SFTP protocol enabled in the bucket settings
explanation: AWS Transfer Family provides fully managed SFTP (and FTPS, FTP) endpoints backed by S3 or EFS. Partners continue using SFTP with their existing clients/tools. Files land in S3 or EFS automatically. Transfer Family handles SFTP protocol, authentication (SSH keys, password), and high availability. Partners see no change in the interface, just a potentially different hostname.

question: A company runs a web application with ALB and EC2. Peak traffic occurs on weekdays 9 AM - 5 PM. Currently using On-Demand instances. They want to reduce costs while ensuring capacity. The peak traffic requires 10 instances and off-peak requires 2 instances. What is the optimal purchase strategy?
options: Buy 10 Reserved Instances to cover peak; no savings outside peak
Buy 2 Reserved Instances for baseline; use On-Demand for peak (8 additional)
Buy 2 Reserved Instances for baseline; use Spot for peak (8 additional)
Use Compute Savings Plans for all 10 instances
explanation: Optimal strategy: 2 Reserved Instances cover the always-running baseline (maximum discount for guaranteed capacity). During peak hours, 8 On-Demand instances are added and terminated after peak. This avoids paying for unused RI capacity off-peak. Spot could save more but risks interruption during peak business hours, making On-Demand the reliable choice for the peak portion.

question: A company is deploying a highly available architecture with an ALB, Auto Scaling group, and RDS Multi-AZ. The architect wants to ensure that if one entire AWS Availability Zone fails, the application remains fully operational. What additional configuration is needed?
options: Deploy EC2 instances in at least 3 AZs with minimum 1 instance per AZ
Enable ALB cross-zone load balancing
Configure RDS Multi-AZ to span 3 AZs
Deploy the ALB in all AZs where EC2 instances run
explanation: For AZ failure resilience, you need instances in at least 3 AZs with a minimum desired count that ensures at least 1 instance remains in each of the surviving AZs when one fails. With 3 AZs and minimum 1 instance per AZ (minimum 3 total), losing one AZ leaves 2 instances serving traffic. The ALB automatically stops routing to the failed AZ, and Auto Scaling compensates.

question: A company wants to use machine learning to detect fraudulent transactions in real-time as they occur. Each transaction must be evaluated within 100ms. Which AWS architecture meets this requirement?
options: Batch ML inference on Redshift data every hour using SageMaker batch transform
API Gateway + Lambda calling SageMaker endpoint for real-time inference per transaction
Kinesis Data Streams + Lambda calling pre-trained SageMaker endpoint
SageMaker Studio notebook with scheduled hourly fraud detection runs
explanation: For real-time, sub-100ms inference, deploy the ML model as a SageMaker real-time endpoint. Transaction processing code (API Gateway + Lambda or direct EC2 call) invokes the endpoint synchronously per transaction. SageMaker endpoints provide auto-scaling for variable request volumes and typically respond in milliseconds, well within the 100ms requirement.

question: A company uses CloudFormation to manage infrastructure. They want to share common components (VPC, subnets, security groups) across multiple CloudFormation stacks to avoid duplication. What CloudFormation feature enables this?
options: CloudFormation Stack Policies to protect shared resources
CloudFormation Cross-Stack References with Outputs and ImportValue
CloudFormation Nested Stacks that embed the shared template
CloudFormation StackSets to deploy to multiple accounts
explanation: CloudFormation Cross-Stack References allow one stack to export values (VPC ID, subnet IDs, security group IDs) using the Outputs section with Export. Other stacks import these values using the Fn::ImportValue function. This creates a dependency relationship where the consuming stack uses the shared resources without duplicating their definition.

question: A company has a primary database in us-east-1 and wants to make it available in eu-west-1 for disaster recovery with an RPO of 1 second. Which database technology supports this?
options: RDS MySQL with cross-region read replica (async replication)
Aurora Global Database with primary in us-east-1 and secondary in eu-west-1
DynamoDB Global Tables with multi-region replication
RDS MySQL with Multi-AZ and cross-region snapshot copy
explanation: Aurora Global Database uses dedicated storage-layer replication that achieves physical replication lag of typically less than 1 second (Aurora's SLA states sub-second). This is different from MySQL binlog replication which has higher latency. Aurora Global Database can have a primary region and up to 5 secondary regions, with RTO of under 1 minute for planned failover.

question: An architecture uses API Gateway with Lambda and DynamoDB. During load testing, DynamoDB returns ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors during write bursts. The writes come in unpredictable bursts. What is the most appropriate solution?
options: Increase DynamoDB write capacity to the peak burst level
Switch DynamoDB to On-Demand mode to handle unpredictable bursts
Add SQS between Lambda and DynamoDB to buffer writes and control throughput
Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling with aggressive scale-up policy
explanation: Adding SQS between Lambda and DynamoDB decouples the write rate from the burst traffic pattern. Lambda writes to SQS (which can absorb millions of messages), and a separate set of Lambda functions reads from SQS and writes to DynamoDB at a controlled, steady rate. This smooths out the burst writes and eliminates throttling without requiring expensive burst-level provisioning or on-demand mode costs.

question: A company runs an application on EC2 that writes log files locally. The instances are terminated after processing jobs. They need a centralized log aggregation solution that collects logs before instance termination. What is the best approach?
options: Use EC2 user data to copy logs to S3 at instance startup
Install CloudWatch Agent to stream logs in real-time to CloudWatch Logs
Use Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks to copy logs to S3 before termination
Mount EFS on all instances and write logs there
explanation: The CloudWatch Agent continuously streams log files from EC2 instances to CloudWatch Logs in near-real-time. Since logs are pushed continuously (not at termination), there's no risk of losing logs when instances are terminated. Using lifecycle hooks (option C) only captures logs at termination time and can fail if the copy takes too long.

question: A company uses AWS Fargate for containerized workloads. Each task requires 4 vCPU and 8 GB memory. Tasks run for an average of 30 minutes and are triggered by SQS messages. During peak hours, 500 tasks run simultaneously. What happens if the Fargate service cannot provision enough tasks?
options: Fargate automatically queues tasks and provisions them as capacity becomes available
Tasks fail immediately with a CapacityProviderException error
SQS messages accumulate and tasks are retried when capacity is available
Fargate limits the number of concurrent tasks based on account limits
explanation: AWS Fargate has account-level concurrency limits and regional capacity limits. If the account limit or regional capacity is exceeded, task launches fail. SQS messages remain in the queue (within retention period), and Lambda or ECS service retry logic can attempt to launch tasks again. You can request Fargate capacity increases through AWS Support and use Fargate capacity reservations.

question: A solutions architect is designing a data pipeline that must process streaming data with exactly-once semantics and maintain the processing state across multiple sessions. Which service combination provides stateful exactly-once stream processing?
options: Kinesis Data Streams + Lambda with SQS DLQ for error handling
Apache Flink on Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink with exactly-once checkpointing
Kinesis Data Analytics SQL queries on sliding windows
SQS FIFO + Lambda with idempotency keys
explanation: Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink (formerly Kinesis Data Analytics for Apache Flink) provides exactly-once processing semantics with checkpointing. Flink maintains stateful computations using managed state backends, persists state to S3 (via checkpoints), and ensures exactly-once end-to-end when reading from Kinesis and writing to supported sinks. It's the only AWS service that provides true stateful exactly-once streaming.

question: A company's EC2 application uses an Amazon Aurora cluster. The application frequently queries for the latest records added in the last 5 minutes. These queries are slow because they scan a large table. What DynamoDB-style approach can be applied to Aurora to optimize these time-based queries?
options: Create a composite index on (created_at) column
Use Aurora parallel query feature for time-range queries
Partition the Aurora table by created_at and create a composite index
Use Aurora Serverless for auto-optimized query execution
explanation: Table partitioning by time (e.g., monthly or daily partitions) combined with a composite index on the partition key and created_at allows queries for recent records to scan only the current partition instead of the entire table. MySQL/Aurora supports RANGE partitioning by date, dramatically reducing the rows scanned for time-based queries on large tables.

question: A company needs to implement a geographically distributed content distribution system where users always access the nearest server. The servers must share a common IP address space that doesn't change from the user's perspective. Which technology enables this?
options: Route 53 latency-based routing with Elastic IPs
CloudFront with multiple regional origins
AWS Global Accelerator with static anycast IP addresses
Route 53 geoproximity routing with biased weights
explanation: AWS Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IP addresses that are announced from all AWS edge locations simultaneously. Traffic is automatically routed to the nearest healthy endpoint. From the user's perspective, the IP never changes regardless of which regional server handles the request. This is true anycast, unlike Route 53's DNS-based approach which has propagation delays.

question: A company's application uses API Gateway REST API. Clients send large JSON payloads (100-500 KB) per request. The team wants to reduce the payload size transmitted over the network. What API Gateway feature enables this?
options: API Gateway content encoding with gzip compression
API Gateway request validation to reject large payloads
API Gateway stage-level caching for repeated large payloads
API Gateway usage plans with payload size throttling
explanation: API Gateway supports gzip content encoding. When clients send the Accept-Encoding: gzip header, API Gateway compresses the response payload. For large JSON payloads, gzip typically achieves 70-90% compression, significantly reducing data transfer size. This is particularly impactful for bandwidth-constrained clients or high-volume APIs where data transfer costs matter.

question: A company uses Amazon SQS to decouple producers and consumers. They want to ensure that messages older than 1 hour are not processed because they are no longer relevant. What SQS feature should be used?
options: Set the message visibility timeout to 1 hour
Set the message retention period to 1 hour
Use a Lambda filter to check message age before processing
Configure a DLQ with 1-hour retention to capture expired messages
explanation: The SQS message retention period determines how long SQS keeps messages. Setting it to 1 hour (3600 seconds) causes SQS to automatically delete messages older than 1 hour. However, this is a blunt approach. For more precise message-age filtering, consumers can check the message's SentTimestamp attribute and discard old messages, combined with the retention period as a safety net.

question: A company has an application that uses Amazon S3 to store millions of objects. Users frequently request the same objects but the cache hit rate on CloudFront is low because each user request includes a unique session token as a query string parameter. How should CloudFront be configured to fix this?
options: Disable CloudFront query string forwarding for all parameters
Configure CloudFront cache policy to exclude the session token query string from the cache key
Use a Lambda@Edge function to strip the session token before caching
Enable CloudFront signed URLs which override the session token
explanation: CloudFront cache policies define what's included in the cache key. By default, all query strings are part of the cache key, so ?token=user1_xxx and ?token=user2_xxx are treated as different objects. Configuring the cache policy to exclude the session token from the cache key ensures CloudFront treats these as the same cacheable object, dramatically improving cache hit rates.
